name: Promethium
name_origin: Named after Prometheus, the Greek titan who stole fire for humanity, reflecting both the daring of its synthesis and its radioactive nature.

discovery.discoverer: Jacob A. Marinsky, Lawrence E. Glendenin, and Charles D. Coryell

occurrence.abundance_summary: Essentially absent from Earth's crust; only trace amounts (~10^-15 g per kg) occur naturally as products of spontaneous fission of U-238 in pitchblende. Produced artificially as a fission product in nuclear reactors.

compounds.name: Promethium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale pink-to-lavender solid; the most studied promethium compound, used as a starting material for other promethium chemistry.

compounds.name: Promethium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Lavender-coloured salt with a hexagonal UCl3-type structure; used in nuclear research.

compounds.name: Promethium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Pink solid used historically in research-scale luminous paint formulations.

compounds.name: Promethium(III) nitrate
compounds.summary: Water-soluble pink salt used as a soluble source of promethium ions in radiochemical separations.

uses: Beta-emitting power source in long-life nuclear batteries (Pm-147)
Luminous paint for watch dials (largely phased out due to radioactivity)
Thickness-gauge measurement of paper, plastic, and metal sheet
Portable X-ray source when combined with a target
Research into rare-earth chemistry and lanthanide spectroscopy
reactivity_summary: A silvery, highly radioactive metal whose chemistry resembles other trivalent lanthanides; reacts with air, water (slowly cold, faster hot), and acids. All bulk samples are warm to the touch from radioactive self-heating and emit a faint blue-green glow due to beta-induced fluorescence.
isotopes_summary: Promethium has no stable isotopes. The most stable are Pm-145 (half-life 17.7 years), Pm-146 (5.53 years), and Pm-147 (2.62 years), the last being the most commercially important and produced as a uranium fission product.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Promethium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
National Nuclear Data Center - NuDat 3 (Brookhaven National Laboratory)
